1→# Outbound Signals Reference
     2→
     3→Signals are data points that tell you whether a prospect is worth reaching out to, when, and how. Nurge's research and qualification skills use these signals to build prospect dossiers and score leads.
     4→
     5→**Key principle:** Most signals are findable via web search and native Claude tools (WebSearch, WebFetch). No paid APIs required for the core signal set. Enrichment connectors (ContactOut, Apollo, Clay) add structured data on top.
     6→
     7→**Parallel execution:** When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch multiple agents in parallel — one per signal category. Each agent runs its own web searches independently and reports back. This turns a 5-minute serial research task into a 30-second parallel one.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Signal Categories
    12→
    13→### 1. Buying Signals (They Might Need What You Sell)
    14→
    15→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    16→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    17→| **Recently raised funding** | New budget, growth mandate, likely hiring and buying tools | WebSearch: "[company] funding round 2025 2026", Crunchbase, TechCrunch, company blog |
    18→| **Hiring for relevant roles** | Building a team = buying tools for that team | WebSearch: "[company] hiring [role]", company careers page (WebFetch /careers), LinkedIn jobs, Greenhouse/Lever pages |
    19→| **Job posts mention your problem space** | Explicit signal they have the pain you solve | WebSearch: "[company] jobs [keyword from your product]", Indeed, LinkedIn |
    20→| **Tech stack changes** | Migrating or adopting new tech = buying window | WebSearch: "[company] migrates to [tech]", BuiltWith, StackShare, GitHub repos |
    21→| **Headcount growth** | Growing company = growing needs | WebSearch: "[company] headcount growth", LinkedIn company page (via search) |
    22→| **New leadership** | New CTO/VP often brings new tools, new strategy | WebSearch: "[company] new CTO/VP/Head of", LinkedIn (via search) |
    23→| **Expanding to new markets** | New geo/vertical = new infrastructure needs | WebSearch: "[company] expansion", press releases, company blog |
    24→| **Product launch** | Shipping something new = needs around that launch | WebSearch: "[company] launches", Product Hunt, company blog |
    25→| **Partnerships announced** | Signals growth and strategic direction | WebSearch: "[company] partnership", press releases |
    26→| **Pain signals in reviews** | Users complaining about problems you solve | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]", "site:glassdoor.com [company]", Capterra reviews |
    27→| **Competitor churn** | They're leaving a competitor = active buyer | WebSearch: "[company] switches from [competitor]", G2 reviews mentioning competitors |
    28→| **RFP/procurement signals** | Actively buying | WebSearch: "[company] RFP [category]", government procurement sites |
    29→
    30→### 2. Timing Signals (Right Moment to Reach Out)
    31→
    32→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    33→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    34→| **Fiscal year start** | New budget cycle = spending authority | WebSearch: "[company] fiscal year", SEC filings for public companies |
    35→| **End of quarter** | Budget pressure to spend remaining allocation | Calendar-based (Q1: Jan-Mar, Q2: Apr-Jun, etc.) |
    36→| **Contract renewal period** | Evaluating alternatives | Hard to find externally — infer from when they adopted current tool |
    37→| **Competitor incident** | Their current vendor failed them | WebSearch: "[competitor] outage/breach/issue", Twitter/X, Hacker News |
    38→| **Industry regulation change** | Compliance deadline = forced buying | WebSearch: "[industry] regulation 2026", industry publications |
    39→| **Conference/event season** | Decision-makers are thinking about the space | WebSearch: "[industry] conference 2026", event websites |
    40→| **Recent leadership change** | New leader = 90-day window to make changes | WebSearch: "[company] appoints [role]", LinkedIn |
    41→| **Recent layoffs** | May be looking to do more with less (efficiency tools) | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs", LinkedIn |
    42→
    43→### 3. Fit Signals (They Match Your ICP)
    44→
    45→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    46→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    47→| **Company size** | Within your ICP range | WebSearch: "[company] employees", LinkedIn company page, Crunchbase |
    48→| **Industry/vertical** | Matches your target market | Company website (WebFetch), LinkedIn, Crunchbase |
    49→| **Revenue range** | Can afford your product | WebSearch: "[company] revenue", Crunchbase, SEC filings, Inc 5000, Deloitte Fast 500 |
    50→| **Growth stage** | Startup/growth/enterprise matches your sweet spot | Crunchbase (funding rounds), LinkedIn headcount, press coverage volume |
    51→| **Geography** | Within your target regions | Company website, LinkedIn, WebSearch |
    52→| **Technology used** | Uses tech your product integrates with or replaces | BuiltWith (WebFetch), StackShare, GitHub, job postings (tech requirements) |
    53→| **Business model** | B2B/B2C/SaaS/marketplace — matches your target | Company website (WebFetch) |
    54→| **Customer profile** | Who THEY sell to tells you about their sophistication | Company website, case studies, G2 profile |
    55→
    56→### 4. Persona Signals (About the Individual)
    57→
    58→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    59→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    60→| **Recent job change** | New role = looking to make impact, open to new tools | WebSearch: "[person] joins [company]", LinkedIn (via search) |
    61→| **Published content** | Know what they think about, how they communicate | WebSearch: "[person] blog/article/podcast", LinkedIn posts (via search), Medium, Substack |
    62→| **Conference speaker** | Thought leader, influences decisions | WebSearch: "[person] speaker [conference]", YouTube, event sites |
    63→| **Open source contributor** | Technical, values transparency | GitHub profile, WebSearch: "[person] github" |
    64→| **Social media activity** | What they share = what they care about | WebSearch: "[person] twitter/linkedin", LinkedIn posts (via search) |
    65→| **Book/course author** | Deep expertise in specific area, known authority | WebSearch: "[person] book/course", Amazon |
    66→| **Board/advisory roles** | Influential, connected | WebSearch: "[person] advisor/board", LinkedIn (via search) |
    67→| **Education background** | Shared connections, communication style | LinkedIn (via search), university alumni pages |
    68→| **Mutual connections** | Warm intro potential | Hard via web search — better with LinkedIn Sales Nav (future connector) |
    69→
    70→### 5. Risk Signals (Don't Reach Out / Proceed with Caution)
    71→
    72→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    73→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    74→| **Competitor** | They sell the same thing you do | WebSearch: "[company] vs [your product]", company website (WebFetch), G2 category |
    75→| **Already a customer** | Don't cold email your own customers | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data if connected |
    76→| **Previously rejected** | Respect the no — or wait significant time | `nurge emails list --format json` (check for negative replies) |
    77→| **Unsubscribed/bounced** | Legal obligation to not contact | Suppression list in nurge-hygiene |
    78→| **Company in trouble** | Layoffs, bankruptcy, acquisition = not buying | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs/bankruptcy/acquired", news |
    79→| **Bad domain reputation** | Their emails might not reach you either | DNS check, but usually not relevant for outbound |
    80→| **Recently contacted by team** | Don't double-tap from multiple people | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data |
    81→| **Litigation/controversy** | Risky to associate with | WebSearch: "[company] lawsuit/controversy" |
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→## Parallel Research Architecture
    86→
    87→When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch **parallel agents** — one per signal category. This is the key to fast, thorough research.
    88→
    89→### Agent Dispatch Pattern
    90→
    91→```
    92→User: "Research Acme Corp / Jane Smith"
    93→         │
    94→         ├─→ Agent 1: COMPANY SIGNALS
    95→         │     WebSearch: "[company] funding"
    96→         │     WebSearch: "[company] hiring"
    97→         │     WebSearch: "[company] news 2026"
    98→         │     WebFetch: company website
    99→         │     WebFetch: company careers page
   100→         │
   101→         ├─→ Agent 2: PERSON SIGNALS
   102→         │     WebSearch: "[person] [company]"
   103→         │     WebSearch: "[person] speaker conference"
   104→         │     WebSearch: "[person] blog article"
   105→         │     WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person] posts"
   106→         │
   107→         ├─→ Agent 3: FIT + RISK CHECK
   108→         │     Compare company against ICP (from config)
   109→         │     Check competitor match (from config)
   110→         │     Check prior emails (nurge emails list)
   111→         │     WebSearch: "[company] vs [our product]"
   112→         │
   113→         ├─→ Agent 4: CONTACT INFO
   114→         │     WebSearch: "[person] email [company]"
   115→         │     WebFetch: company /contact or /mentions-legales or /impressum
   116→         │     WebSearch: "site:contactout.com [person]"
   117→         │     WebSearch: "site:rocketreach.co [person]"
   118→         │
   119→         └─→ Agent 5: TIMING SIGNALS
   120→               WebSearch: "[company] Q1 2026 plans"
   121→               WebSearch: "[competitor of their current tool] issues"
   122→               Check fiscal year / industry events
   123→```
   124→
   125→### All agents return → Combine into Dossier
   126→
   127→```
   128→Prospect Dossier: Jane Smith, CTO @ Acme Corp
   129→──────────────────────────────────────────────
   130→FIT:      Strong match (Series B, 120 employees, SaaS, US)
   131→RISK:     None detected (not a competitor, no prior contact)
   132→BUYING:   Just raised $15M Series B (2 months ago), hiring 3 engineers
   133→TIMING:   New CTO (started 4 months ago), fiscal year starts Jan
   134→PERSONA:  Spoke at DevConf 2025 on "scaling engineering teams",
   135→          posts about developer productivity, values transparency
   136→CONTACT:  jane@acme.com (from careers page), LinkedIn: /in/janesmith
   137→ANGLE:    Reference her DevConf talk on scaling → your product helps
   138→          engineering teams ship faster → relevant to her $15M growth mandate
   139→
   140→CONFIDENCE: High (multiple corroborating sources)
   141→```
   142→
   143→### Present to user for review before drafting.
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Signal Sources Summary
   148→
   149→| Source | Free? | What It Gets You | How to Access |
   150→|--------|-------|-----------------|---------------|
   151→| **Google (WebSearch)** | Yes | Everything — the starting point for all signals | WebSearch tool |
   152→| **Company website** | Yes | Product info, team, careers, legal pages (contact info) | WebFetch tool |
   153→| **LinkedIn posts** | Yes | What prospects think about, recent activity | WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person]" |
   154→| **LinkedIn profile** | Blocked | Direct scrape fails (403/999) | Use web search to get profile snippets |
   155→| **Crunchbase** | Partial | Funding, investors, company info | WebSearch (basic), WebFetch (some pages) |
   156→| **G2** | Yes | Reviews, competitor comparisons, pain signals | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]" |
   157→| **Glassdoor** | Yes | Company culture, leadership sentiment | WebSearch: "site:glassdoor.com [company]" |
   158→| **GitHub** | Yes | Tech stack, open source activity, contributor profiles | WebSearch/WebFetch |
   159→| **Product Hunt** | Yes | Product launches, tech adoption | WebSearch: "site:producthunt.com [company]" |
   160→| **Twitter/X** | Partial | Real-time signals, opinions, announcements | WebSearch: "site:twitter.com [person/company]" |
   161→| **News (TechCrunch, etc.)** | Yes | Funding, launches, leadership changes | WebSearch |
   162→| **Job boards** | Yes | Hiring signals, tech stack from requirements | WebSearch, WebFetch careers pages |
   163→| **SEC/EDGAR** | Yes | Revenue, financials (public companies only) | WebSearch, WebFetch |
   164→| **Mentions Légales** | Yes | Contact info (French companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].fr/mentionslegales |
   165→| **Impressum** | Yes | Contact info (German companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].de/impressum |
   166→| **ContactOut** | Paid | Verified email + phone from LinkedIn URL | Future connector |
   167→| **Apollo** | Freemium | Email, company data, technographics | Future connector |
   168→| **Clay** | Paid | Waterfall enrichment from multiple sources | Future connector |
   169→| **BuiltWith** | Freemium | Technology stack detection | WebFetch (basic), future connector |
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## How Skills Use Signals
   174→
   175→| Skill | Which Signals | How |
   176→|-------|--------------|-----|
   177→| **nurge-research** | All of them — builds the full dossier | Dispatches parallel agents per category |
   178→| **nurge-qualify** | Fit + Risk + Buying | Scores against ICP, checks competitor, checks BANT |
   179→| **nurge-outbound** | Persona + Timing + Buying | Uses signals to personalize the email angle |
   180→| **nurge-sequence** | Timing | Determines sequence cadence and urgency |
   181→| **nurge-followup** | Buying + Timing | References trigger events in follow-ups |
   182→| **nurge-review** | All (aggregated) | Surfaces which signal types correlate with replies |

file.content:
# Outbound Signals Reference

Signals are data points that tell you whether a prospect is worth reaching out to, when, and how. Nurge's research and qualification skills use these signals to build prospect dossiers and score leads.

**Key principle:** Most signals are findable via web search and native Claude tools (WebSearch, WebFetch). No paid APIs required for the core signal set. Enrichment connectors (ContactOut, Apollo, Clay) add structured data on top.

**Parallel execution:** When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch multiple agents in parallel — one per signal category. Each agent runs its own web searches independently and reports back. This turns a 5-minute serial research task into a 30-second parallel one.

---

## Signal Categories

### 1. Buying Signals (They Might Need What You Sell)

| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Recently raised funding** | New budget, growth mandate, likely hiring and buying tools | WebSearch: "[company] funding round 2025 2026", Crunchbase, TechCrunch, company blog |
| **Hiring for relevant roles** | Building a team = buying tools for that team | WebSearch: "[company] hiring [role]", company careers page (WebFetch /careers), LinkedIn jobs, Greenhouse/Lever pages |
| **Job posts mention your problem space** | Explicit signal they have the pain you solve | WebSearch: "[company] jobs [keyword from your product]", Indeed, LinkedIn |
| **Tech stack changes** | Migrating or adopting new tech = buying window | WebSearch: "[company] migrates to [tech]", BuiltWith, StackShare, GitHub repos |
| **Headcount growth** | Growing company = growing needs | WebSearch: "[company] headcount growth", LinkedIn company page (via search) |
| **New leadership** | New CTO/VP often brings new tools, new strategy | WebSearch: "[company] new CTO/VP/Head of", LinkedIn (via search) |
| **Expanding to new markets** | New geo/vertical = new infrastructure needs | WebSearch: "[company] expansion", press releases, company blog |
| **Product launch** | Shipping something new = needs around that launch | WebSearch: "[company] launches", Product Hunt, company blog |
| **Partnerships announced** | Signals growth and strategic direction | WebSearch: "[company] partnership", press releases |
| **Pain signals in reviews** | Users complaining about problems you solve | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]", "site:glassdoor.com [company]", Capterra reviews |
| **Competitor churn** | They're leaving a competitor = active buyer | WebSearch: "[company] switches from [competitor]", G2 reviews mentioning competitors |
| **RFP/procurement signals** | Actively buying | WebSearch: "[company] RFP [category]", government procurement sites |

### 2. Timing Signals (Right Moment to Reach Out)

| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Fiscal year start** | New budget cycle = spending authority | WebSearch: "[company] fiscal year", SEC filings for public companies |
| **End of quarter** | Budget pressure to spend remaining allocation | Calendar-based (Q1: Jan-Mar, Q2: Apr-Jun, etc.) |
| **Contract renewal period** | Evaluating alternatives | Hard to find externally — infer from when they adopted current tool |
| **Competitor incident** | Their current vendor failed them | WebSearch: "[competitor] outage/breach/issue", Twitter/X, Hacker News |
| **Industry regulation change** | Compliance deadline = forced buying | WebSearch: "[industry] regulation 2026", industry publications |
| **Conference/event season** | Decision-makers are thinking about the space | WebSearch: "[industry] conference 2026", event websites |
| **Recent leadership change** | New leader = 90-day window to make changes | WebSearch: "[company] appoints [role]", LinkedIn |
| **Recent layoffs** | May be looking to do more with less (efficiency tools) | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs", LinkedIn |

### 3. Fit Signals (They Match Your ICP)

| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Company size** | Within your ICP range | WebSearch: "[company] employees", LinkedIn company page, Crunchbase |
| **Industry/vertical** | Matches your target market | Company website (WebFetch), LinkedIn, Crunchbase |
| **Revenue range** | Can afford your product | WebSearch: "[company] revenue", Crunchbase, SEC filings, Inc 5000, Deloitte Fast 500 |
| **Growth stage** | Startup/growth/enterprise matches your sweet spot | Crunchbase (funding rounds), LinkedIn headcount, press coverage volume |
| **Geography** | Within your target regions | Company website, LinkedIn, WebSearch |
| **Technology used** | Uses tech your product integrates with or replaces | BuiltWith (WebFetch), StackShare, GitHub, job postings (tech requirements) |
| **Business model** | B2B/B2C/SaaS/marketplace — matches your target | Company website (WebFetch) |
| **Customer profile** | Who THEY sell to tells you about their sophistication | Company website, case studies, G2 profile |

### 4. Persona Signals (About the Individual)

| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Recent job change** | New role = looking to make impact, open to new tools | WebSearch: "[person] joins [company]", LinkedIn (via search) |
| **Published content** | Know what they think about, how they communicate | WebSearch: "[person] blog/article/podcast", LinkedIn posts (via search), Medium, Substack |
| **Conference speaker** | Thought leader, influences decisions | WebSearch: "[person] speaker [conference]", YouTube, event sites |
| **Open source contributor** | Technical, values transparency | GitHub profile, WebSearch: "[person] github" |
| **Social media activity** | What they share = what they care about | WebSearch: "[person] twitter/linkedin", LinkedIn posts (via search) |
| **Book/course author** | Deep expertise in specific area, known authority | WebSearch: "[person] book/course", Amazon |
| **Board/advisory roles** | Influential, connected | WebSearch: "[person] advisor/board", LinkedIn (via search) |
| **Education background** | Shared connections, communication style | LinkedIn (via search), university alumni pages |
| **Mutual connections** | Warm intro potential | Hard via web search — better with LinkedIn Sales Nav (future connector) |

### 5. Risk Signals (Don't Reach Out / Proceed with Caution)

| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Competitor** | They sell the same thing you do | WebSearch: "[company] vs [your product]", company website (WebFetch), G2 category |
| **Already a customer** | Don't cold email your own customers | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data if connected |
| **Previously rejected** | Respect the no — or wait significant time | `nurge emails list --format json` (check for negative replies) |
| **Unsubscribed/bounced** | Legal obligation to not contact | Suppression list in nurge-hygiene |
| **Company in trouble** | Layoffs, bankruptcy, acquisition = not buying | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs/bankruptcy/acquired", news |
| **Bad domain reputation** | Their emails might not reach you either | DNS check, but usually not relevant for outbound |
| **Recently contacted by team** | Don't double-tap from multiple people | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data |
| **Litigation/controversy** | Risky to associate with | WebSearch: "[company] lawsuit/controversy" |

---

## Parallel Research Architecture

When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch **parallel agents** — one per signal category. This is the key to fast, thorough research.

### Agent Dispatch Pattern

```
User: "Research Acme Corp / Jane Smith"
         │
         ├─→ Agent 1: COMPANY SIGNALS
         │     WebSearch: "[company] funding"
         │     WebSearch: "[company] hiring"
         │     WebSearch: "[company] news 2026"
         │     WebFetch: company website
         │     WebFetch: company careers page
         │
         ├─→ Agent 2: PERSON SIGNALS
         │     WebSearch: "[person] [company]"
         │     WebSearch: "[person] speaker conference"
         │     WebSearch: "[person] blog article"
         │     WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person] posts"
         │
         ├─→ Agent 3: FIT + RISK CHECK
         │     Compare company against ICP (from config)
         │     Check competitor match (from config)
         │     Check prior emails (nurge emails list)
         │     WebSearch: "[company] vs [our product]"
         │
         ├─→ Agent 4: CONTACT INFO
         │     WebSearch: "[person] email [company]"
         │     WebFetch: company /contact or /mentions-legales or /impressum
         │     WebSearch: "site:contactout.com [person]"
         │     WebSearch: "site:rocketreach.co [person]"
         │
         └─→ Agent 5: TIMING SIGNALS
               WebSearch: "[company] Q1 2026 plans"
               WebSearch: "[competitor of their current tool] issues"
               Check fiscal year / industry events
```

### All agents return → Combine into Dossier

```
Prospect Dossier: Jane Smith, CTO @ Acme Corp
──────────────────────────────────────────────
FIT:      Strong match (Series B, 120 employees, SaaS, US)
RISK:     None detected (not a competitor, no prior contact)
BUYING:   Just raised $15M Series B (2 months ago), hiring 3 engineers
TIMING:   New CTO (started 4 months ago), fiscal year starts Jan
PERSONA:  Spoke at DevConf 2025 on "scaling engineering teams",
          posts about developer productivity, values transparency
CONTACT:  jane@acme.com (from careers page), LinkedIn: /in/janesmith
ANGLE:    Reference her DevConf talk on scaling → your product helps
          engineering teams ship faster → relevant to her $15M growth mandate

CONFIDENCE: High (multiple corroborating sources)
```

### Present to user for review before drafting.

---

## Signal Sources Summary

| Source | Free? | What It Gets You | How to Access |
|--------|-------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Google (WebSearch)** | Yes | Everything — the starting point for all signals | WebSearch tool |
| **Company website** | Yes | Product info, team, careers, legal pages (contact info) | WebFetch tool |
| **LinkedIn posts** | Yes | What prospects think about, recent activity | WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person]" |
| **LinkedIn profile** | Blocked | Direct scrape fails (403/999) | Use web search to get profile snippets |
| **Crunchbase** | Partial | Funding, investors, company info | WebSearch (basic), WebFetch (some pages) |
| **G2** | Yes | Reviews, competitor comparisons, pain signals | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]" |
| **Glassdoor** | Yes | Company culture, leadership sentiment | WebSearch: "site:glassdoor.com [company]" |
| **GitHub** | Yes | Tech stack, open source activity, contributor profiles | WebSearch/WebFetch |
| **Product Hunt** | Yes | Product launches, tech adoption | WebSearch: "site:producthunt.com [company]" |
| **Twitter/X** | Partial | Real-time signals, opinions, announcements | WebSearch: "site:twitter.com [person/company]" |
| **News (TechCrunch, etc.)** | Yes | Funding, launches, leadership changes | WebSearch |
| **Job boards** | Yes | Hiring signals, tech stack from requirements | WebSearch, WebFetch careers pages |
| **SEC/EDGAR** | Yes | Revenue, financials (public companies only) | WebSearch, WebFetch |
| **Mentions Légales** | Yes | Contact info (French companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].fr/mentionslegales |
| **Impressum** | Yes | Contact info (German companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].de/impressum |
| **ContactOut** | Paid | Verified email + phone from LinkedIn URL | Future connector |
| **Apollo** | Freemium | Email, company data, technographics | Future connector |
| **Clay** | Paid | Waterfall enrichment from multiple sources | Future connector |
| **BuiltWith** | Freemium | Technology stack detection | WebFetch (basic), future connector |

---

## How Skills Use Signals

| Skill | Which Signals | How |
|-------|--------------|-----|
| **nurge-research** | All of them — builds the full dossier | Dispatches parallel agents per category |
| **nurge-qualify** | Fit + Risk + Buying | Scores against ICP, checks competitor, checks BANT |
| **nurge-outbound** | Persona + Timing + Buying | Uses signals to personalize the email angle |
| **nurge-sequence** | Timing | Determines sequence cadence and urgency |
| **nurge-followup** | Buying + Timing | References trigger events in follow-ups |
| **nurge-review** | All (aggregated) | Surfaces which signal types correlate with replies |

input.content:
# Skill Catalog v2: Research-Backed Data Model

**Date:** 2026-03-25
**Status:** Draft
**Context:** Based on research into Lavender, Apollo, Instantly, Autobound, Clay, Gong, Josh Braun, and 2026 cold email benchmarks.

## Problem

The current 17 skills are structurally sound but data-thin. Setup collects enough to generate *functional* skills but not *effective* ones. Research shows the gap between 3.4% reply rates (generic) and 25%+ reply rates (signal-based, voice-matched, objection-aware) comes down to what data the system knows before writing the first email.

## What Changes

- **1 new skill:** `nurge-voice` (sender personality profile)
- **9 enriched skills:** product, icp, personas, outbound, sequence, qualify, replies, analytics, review
- **7 new setup steps** in Phase 2 Zero flow
- **9 new config keys**
- Total: **18 skills** (17 project-local + 1 global setup)
- Core skills generated during setup: **6** (was 5)

## The 18 Skills

### Master Table

| # | Skill | Category | When Generated | What It Knows (Current) | What's New (v2) |
|---|-------|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | `nurge-setup` | Setup | Binary install | Onboarding orchestrator | +7 new collection steps |
| 1 | `nurge-product` | Context | Setup (core) | Company, positioning, social proof | **+competitors, +objections, +pricing stance, +differentiators** |
| 2 | `nurge-icp` | Context | Setup (core) | Company size, industry, stage, geo | **+negative ICP, +trigger signals, +technographics** |
| 3 | `nurge-personas` | Context | Setup (core) | Title, pain, messaging angle | **+per-persona objections, +CTA style, +tone profile, +buying role** |
| 4 | `nurge-voice` | Context | **Setup (core) — NEW** | — | **Writing style, tone, vocabulary, anti-patterns, sign-off, reading level** |
| 5 | `nurge-outbound` | Action | Setup (core) | 50-90 words, structure, filler rules | **+voice matching, +signal hooks, +CTA strategy, +reading level, +subject line rules** |
| 6 | `nurge-pipeline` | Workflow | Setup (core) | KPIs, sending limits | +signal-based prioritization |
| 7 | `nurge-research` | Research | Lazy (first use) | 5-layer parallel dossier | +signal detection, +competitive intel check |
| 8 | `nurge-qualify` | Research | Lazy | BANT, ICP fit, classification | **+disqualification criteria, +signal scoring tiers, +competitor detection** |
| 9 | `nurge-sequence` | Action | Lazy | 3-step cadence | **+architecture (5 touches), +angle rotation, +breakup email, +multi-threading** |
| 10 | `nurge-followup` | Action | Lazy | New-angle follow-ups | +objection-aware angles, +signal-based hooks |
| 11 | `nurge-replies` | Action | Lazy | Reply classification + response | **+competitor mention handling, +objection battlecard, +deflection handling** |
| 12 | `nurge-book` | Action | Lazy | Meeting booking | No change |
| 13 | `nurge-prospects` | Prospects | Lazy | List building, segmentation | +signal filtering, +negative ICP exclusion |
| 14 | `nurge-hygiene` | Health | Lazy | Data cleanup, compliance | No change |
| 15 | `nurge-sending-health` | Health | Lazy | DNS, warmup, bounces | No change |
| 16 | `nurge-analytics` | Measure | Lazy | KPI tracking | **+per-signal reply rate, +per-CTA conversion, +voice consistency** |
| 17 | `nurge-review` | Measure | Lazy | Weekly coaching | **+signal effectiveness, +voice drift, +objection patterns** |

### Generation Order

| Phase | Skills (in order) | Rationale |
|-------|-------------------|-----------|
| Setup (core 6) | product → icp → personas → voice → outbound → pipeline | Knowledge base first, then how to sound, then how to write, then how to orchestrate |
| Lazy (11) | Generated on first invocation | More data available by then = better skills |

---

## Skill Details

### 1. `nurge-product` — Company Battlecard

**Purpose:** Everything Claude needs about the sender's company to write accurate emails and handle conversations.

**Current contents:**
- Company name, website, industry
- One-line positioning for cold outreach
- Value propositions
- Social proof (or "none")

**New contents:**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Top 3-5 competitors** | Skills need competitors to "plant landmines" — features competitors can't match. Never name competitors proactively in emails. | Setup Z7b |
| **Differentiators per competitor** | "When they mention X, we do Y better because Z" — feeds nurge-replies objection handling | Setup Z7b |
| **Objection map (top 5)** | Pre-map every objection with pre-emptive framings. Josh Braun: prevent objections, don't handle them after the fact | Setup Z8b |
| **Pricing stance** | Not the actual price — the *framing*. "$250/mo" vs "replaces a $5K/mo SDR" vs "free trial, no card" | Setup Z7b |
| **What you're NOT** | "We're not a CRM. We're not Instantly." Prevents positioning drift in generated emails | Setup Z7b |

**Config keys:** `company.*`, `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `product.competitors` (JSON), `product.objections` (JSON), `product.pricing_stance`

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-replies, nurge-followup, nurge-qualify, nurge-research

---

### 2. `nurge-icp` — Who to Target (and Who Not To)

**Purpose:** Defines the ideal customer profile — which companies to target, which to avoid, and what signals indicate they're ready to buy.

**Current contents:**
- Company size, industry, stage, geography

**New contents:**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Negative ICP (disqualification)** | Characteristics that predict churn, slow cycles, or low LTV. Hard stop, not deprioritization. Apollo data: negative ICP is as valuable as positive ICP | Setup Z5b |
| **Trigger events / buying signals** | Signal-based outreach = 15-25% reply rates vs 3.4% generic. The single biggest lever for reply rates. Autobound: 644 signals from 29 sources | Setup Z6b |
| **Signal priority ranking** | Which signals indicate highest intent for *this specific* product. Funding matters more for some products, hiring for others | Setup Z6b |
| **Technographics** | Tech stack signals (uses React, runs on AWS, has Salesforce) indicate fit and determine approach angle | Setup Z5 (enriched) |
| **Timing windows** | Autobound data: reaching out within 48 hours of a trigger = 5x more likely to win. Define urgency per signal type | Setup Z6b |

**Config keys:** `icp.*`, `icp.negative` (JSON), `icp.signals` (JSON array), `icp.technographics`, `prospects.geography`

**Used by:** nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects, nurge-outbound, nurge-pipeline, nurge-analytics

---

### 3. `nurge-personas` — Who to Message and How

**Purpose:** Buyer personas — which people to message within target companies, and how to message each one differently.

**Current contents (per persona):**
- Title/role patterns
- Pain points
- Messaging angle

**New contents (per persona):**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-persona objections** | CTOs object differently than VPs. "Too technical" vs "not proven enough" vs "we already use X" | Derived from objection map + persona during setup |
| **Per-persona CTA style** | Gong data: interest CTAs ("worth a look?") outperform meeting CTAs. But CTOs may prefer low-friction while VPs may respond to specific times | Setup (CTA step) |
| **Tone profile** | Technical/casual for CTOs, business/formal for VPs. Lavender: tone match to recipient personality = higher replies | Setup |
| **Buying role** | Champion (sells internally), Decision-maker (signs off), Blocker (can kill deal), Influencer (shapes opinion). Changes the ask | Setup Z6 (enriched) |
| **Per-persona positioning** | Different one-liner per persona. Already happened organically in the test onboarding — formalize it | Setup Z7 (enriched) |

**Config keys:** `personas` (enriched JSON array — each object gains: `objections`, `cta_style`, `tone`, `buying_role`, `positioning`)

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-sequence, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-book, nurge-research

---

### 4. `nurge-voice` — Sender Personality Profile (NEW)

**Purpose:** Makes every email sound like the user wrote it, not like AI. This is Nurge's long-term moat — the voice model gets better with every interaction.

**What it contains:**

| Data Point | How It's Extracted | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Formality level** | Analyze sent emails for contractions, greetings, structure | "Casual — contractions, first names, no 'Dear'" |
| **Sentence structure** | Avg sentence length, complexity, fragments | "Short sentences. Rarely uses semicolons. Fragments OK." |
| **Vocabulary patterns** | Frequently used words and phrases | "Says 'ship' not 'deliver'. Says 'worth a look' not 'I'd love to schedule'" |
| **Anti-patterns** | Things the user would NEVER say | "Never: 'Hope this finds you well', 'Just circling back', 'Per my last email'" |
| **Opener style** | How they start emails | "Jumps straight in. No greeting pleasantries. First name only." |
| **Closer style** | How they sign off | "Just first name. No title. No phone number in signature." |
| **Punctuation / emoji** | Habits | "Uses em dashes. No emojis. Occasional '...' for trailing thoughts" |
| **Reading level** | Lavender data: 3rd-5th grade reading level = 67% more replies | "Grade 4 — simple words, short sentences" |
| **I/You ratio** | Lavender data: more 'you' language = more replies | "Target: 3:1 you-to-I ratio" |
| **Humor / personality** | Whether they're playful, dry, or strictly professional | "Dry, direct. Occasional wit. Never cheesy or forced." |

**How it's built:**
1. **During setup (Z10b):** Analyze classified outbound + personal emails for patterns. Even 1 outbound + 10 personal emails yield a useful baseline.
2. **Over time:** Every draft the user edits, rejects, or approves refines the profile. Rejected phrasings become anti-patterns. Accepted ones reinforce the model. This is the "Whoop effect" — the system gets better the longer you use it.

**Config keys:** `voice.formality`, `voice.sentence_style`, `voice.vocabulary`, `voice.anti_patterns`, `voice.opener`, `voice.closer`, `voice.reading_level`, `voice.personality` (or single `voice` JSON object)

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-sequence, nurge-book, nurge-review (drift detection)

---

### 5. `nurge-outbound` — Write Cold Emails

**Purpose:** Draft personalized cold emails. The highest-priority action skill.

**Current rules:**
- 50-90 word hard limit
- Structure: 1 hook → 1-2 value → 1 CTA
- No filler words
- Persona-specific tone
- Social proof weaving (if exists)

**New rules:**

| Rule | Research Backing |
|---|---|
| **Match sender voice profile** | Lavender: emails matching sender's natural voice get 4x reply rates. Reference nurge-voice for every draft |
| **Signal-based hook (sentence 1)** | Autobound: signal-based personalization = 15-25% reply rate. "Saw you just raised Series B" > "Hi, I'm from Company X" |
| **Interest CTA by default** | Gong: "Worth a look?" outperforms "Free Tuesday at 2?" — asking for time triggers loss aversion. Override per persona if configured |
| **Reading level: grade 3-5** | Lavender: 3rd-5th grade reading level = 67% more replies. Simple words, short sentences |
| **I/You ratio: 3:1 you-to-I** | Lavender: more "you" = more replies. Never open a sentence with "I" |
| **Subject line: 36-50 chars** | Instantly benchmark: 36-50 char subjects = highest response. Numbers boost open rate 113% |
| **Competitor-aware positioning** | Never name competitors. Plant "landmines" — features they can't match. Reference nurge-product.competitors |
| **25-50 words is the new sweet spot** | Lavender 2026 data: 25-50 words optimal. Our 50-90 range may be too generous. Consider tightening. |

**Updated email structure:**
```
1. Signal-based hook (1 sentence) — references a trigger event or specific observation about the prospect
2. Value prop (1-2 sentences) — connects THEIR situation to YOUR solution. Focus on outcome, not features
3. CTA (1 sentence) — interest-based by default. Low friction. Close-ended question
Total: 50-90 words. Reading level: grade 3-5. Voice: match sender profile from nurge-voice.
```

**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `product.competitors`, `personas`, `voice.*`, `icp.signals`, `prospects.geography`, `sending.*`, `cta.default_style`

---

### 6. `nurge-pipeline` — Daily Orchestrator

**Purpose:** Manages the full outbound workflow, decides next actions, coordinates all skills.

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Signal-based prioritization** | Prospects with active signals go first. 2-3 stacked signals = highest priority tier. Autobound: multi-signal = 25-40% reply rate |
| **Signal freshness decay** | Funding from yesterday > funding from 3 weeks ago. 48-hour window = 5x conversion. Show signal age |
| **Sequence health check** | "5 prospects stuck at step 2 for >7 days" — suggest moving on or trying different angle |

**Prioritization tiers (from Autobound framework):**

| Tier | Signals | Action | Expected Reply Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 (Hot) | 2-3+ active signals | Immediate, fully personalized, assign to best outreach | 25-40% |
| Tier 2 (Warm) | 1 active signal | Semi-personalized cadence | 10-15% |
| Tier 3 (Cold) | 0 signals, fits ICP | Batch cadence, lighter personalization | 3-5% |

---

### 7. `nurge-research` — Prospect Intelligence

**Purpose:** Deep-dive research on a target company or prospect before writing outreach.

**Current:** 5-layer parallel research (web, website, enrichment, public profiles, prior interactions).

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Signal detection** | During research, actively look for trigger events matching `icp.signals`. Flag which signals are active and how fresh they are |
| **Competitive intel check** | Check if prospect uses a competitor (from `product.competitors`). Changes the messaging angle entirely — displacement vs greenfield |
| **Signal freshness** | Always flag recency: "Raised $5M (2 weeks ago)" vs "Raised $5M (6 months ago)". Stale signals ≠ no signals, but urgency drops |
| **Personality indicators** | From LinkedIn posts, podcast appearances, blog writing — communication style clues to help voice-match the email |

---

### 8. `nurge-qualify` — Score and Filter

**Purpose:** Evaluate whether a lead is worth pursuing. Classify, score, and route.

**Current:** Classify (prospect/competitor/partnership) → BANT scoring.

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Disqualification criteria** | Explicit "do NOT pursue" from `icp.negative`. Hard stop, not deprioritization. Apollo: negative ICP prevents wasted cycles |
| **Signal scoring** | 0 signals = cold, 1 = warm, 2+ = hot. Determines outreach tier, personalization level, and urgency |
| **Competitor user detection** | If they use a competitor → displacement opportunity (different messaging) vs if they use nothing → greenfield opportunity |
| **Budget reality check** | Apollo: firmographic match ≠ ability to buy. Check for budget signals (funding, hiring, growth rate) |

**Updated output tiers:**

| Tier | Criteria | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot** | 2+ active signals, strong ICP fit | Immediate outreach, full personalization, deep research |
| **Warm** | 1 active signal or strong ICP fit | Semi-personalized sequence within the week |
| **Cold** | ICP fit but no signals | Batch cadence, monitor for signals |
| **Nurture** | Good fit but bad timing | Park and monitor. Re-qualify when signals appear |
| **Disqualified** | Matches negative ICP or risk signal | Do not email. Log reason |

---

### 9. `nurge-sequence` — Multi-Touch Cadences

**Purpose:** Design and manage multi-step outreach sequences.

**Current:** Simple 3-touch sequence.

**New: Full sequence architecture.**

| Addition | Research Backing |
|---|---|
| **5-touch default** | Instantly: 58% of positive replies from email #1, but 60% of ALL positives come from follow-ups. More touches = more chances |
| **Angle rotation** | Each touch uses a DIFFERENT angle. Never "just following up." New pain point, new proof, new framing per touch |
| **Breakup email (touch 5)** | Josh Braun: low-pressure exit ("Not the right time? No problem.") sometimes triggers the reply that direct asks didn't |
| **Multi-threading** | Same company, different person. If CTO doesn't reply after 3 touches, try VP Eng. Apollo approach |
| **Timing by persona** | CTOs: shorter sequence (3 touches, tighter spacing). VPs: standard (5 touches). SDRs: fastest cadence |

**Default sequence architecture:**

| Touch | Day | Purpose | Angle | Word Count |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Day 0 | Signal-based hook + primary value prop | Primary pain point | 50-90 |
| 2 | Day 3 | New angle, different value | Secondary pain point or proof | 40-70 |
| 3 | Day 7 | Social proof or case study | Third-party validation | 40-70 |
| 4 | Day 14 | Direct ask or escalate | More specific/direct CTA | 30-50 |
| 5 | Day 21 | Breakup | "No worries if not — just wanted to flag it" | 20-40 |

**Config it reads:** `personas`, `kpi.*`, `sending.*`, `voice.*`, `product.social_proof`, `icp.signals`, `sequence.architecture`, `cta.default_style`

---

### 10. `nurge-followup` — Non-Response Follow-Ups

**Purpose:** Write follow-up emails for prospects who haven't replied.

**Enriched with:**
- **Genuinely new angle per touch** — not restating email #1. Different pain point, different proof, different framing
- **Voice consistency** — must match nurge-voice profile
- **Signal-based hooks** — if a NEW signal has occurred since last touch, lead with it ("Just saw you launched X...")
- **Shorter than initial** — follow-ups should be 30-50 words, not 50-90
- **Objection-aware** — if the silence pattern suggests a likely objection (e.g., pricing), pre-emptively address it

---

### 11. `nurge-replies` — Handle Incoming Responses

**Purpose:** Classify and respond to incoming replies.

**Current:** Classify (interested/objection/not now/not interested/referral/OOO) + draft response.

**New: Battlecard-powered objection handling.**

| Reply Type | New Handling | Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **"We use [competitor]"** | Pull competitor battlecard from nurge-product. Respond with differentiators. Never trash the competitor — acknowledge they're good, then differentiate | `product.competitors` |
| **"Too expensive / no budget"** | Pull pricing stance. Reframe value ("replaces a $5K/mo SDR"), don't discount. Offer a trial if available | `product.pricing_stance`, `product.objections` |
| **"Not the right time"** | Acknowledge + ask "when would be better?" Schedule nurture follow-up. Don't push | `product.objections` |
| **"Send me more info"** | This is usually a deflection. Send a one-pager, not a feature dump. Ask a qualifying question back to test real interest | `product.objections` |
| **"How is this different from X?"** | Pull specific differentiators. Be honest about where competitor is stronger — builds credibility | `product.competitors` |
| **Positive reply** | Move to booking. Match their energy/tone. Don't oversell — they already said yes | Standard |

---

### 12. `nurge-book` — Meeting Booking

No changes. Current design is solid.

---

### 13. `nurge-prospects` — List Building & CRM

**New additions:**
- **Signal-based filtering:** Prioritize prospects with active trigger events from `icp.signals`
- **Negative ICP exclusion:** Auto-filter against `icp.negative` disqualification criteria
- **Signal stacking score:** Show signal count per prospect for prioritization display

---

### 14. `nurge-hygiene` — Data Cleanup

No changes needed.

### 15. `nurge-sending-health` — Infrastructure Monitoring

No changes needed.

---

### 16. `nurge-analytics` — Performance Tracking

**New metrics:**

| Metric | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Reply rate by signal type** | "Funding signals → 22% reply rate, hiring signals → 8%." Invest research time where signals actually work |
| **Reply rate by CTA type** | "Interest CTAs → 12%, specific time CTAs → 6%." Optimize CTA strategy per persona |
| **Voice consistency score** | Are generated emails drifting from the sender's profile? Flag drift before it becomes a pattern |
| **Per-competitor win/loss** | When prospects use competitor X, how often do we convert? Informs competitive positioning |
| **Signal ROI** | Which signals are worth the research time vs noise. Stop chasing signals that don't convert |

---

### 17. `nurge-review` — Weekly Coaching

**New coaching areas:**

| Area | What It Does |
|---|---|
| **Signal effectiveness** | "This week, funding signals drove 3 replies but hiring signals drove 0. Deprioritize hiring signal research." |
| **Voice drift detection** | "Your last 5 emails are more formal than your usual style. Intentional?" |
| **Objection pattern detection** | "3 prospects said 'too expensive' this week — consider adjusting pricing framing in initial emails" |
| **Competitor intelligence** | "2 prospects mentioned using [competitor] this week. Here's what worked / didn't in those conversations" |
| **Sequence optimization** | "Touch 2 is getting 0 replies. The angle isn't working — here are 3 alternatives" |

---

## New Setup Steps (Phase 2 Zero)

These steps are inserted into the existing Zero flow:

| New Step | Insert After | What to Collect | Time Added |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z5b: Negative ICP** | Z5 (ICP) | "Who should you NOT waste time on?" AI suggests based on product + ICP. Company types, persona anti-patterns, deal-breakers | ~1 min |
| **Z6b: Trigger signals** | Z6 (Personas) | "What signals tell you a company is ready to buy right now?" AI suggests top 5 based on product. User confirms/ranks | ~2 min |
| **Z7b: Competitive landscape** | Z7 (Positioning) | "Who else solves this problem?" Top 3-5 competitors or alternatives. Differentiators. "What do you do better?" Pricing stance | ~2 min |
| **Z8b: Objection map** | Z8 (Social proof) | "What pushback do you expect when you reach out?" AI suggests top 5 based on product + competitors. Pre-emptive framings | ~1 min |
| **Z9: CTA + sequence strategy** | Replaces old Z9 (BANT) | Default CTA style. Sequence length preference. Breakup style. Replace BANT (useless for zero users) with something actionable | ~1 min |
| **Z10b: Voice extraction** | Before Z11 (Skills) | Analyze classified outbound + personal emails for writing patterns. Present profile for user review | ~1 min |

**Total added time:** ~8 minutes. Total setup: ~13 minutes (was ~5).

**Tradeoff:** 8 extra minutes during setup → dramatically better skills from day 1. Every email after setup benefits.

---

## New Config Keys

| Key | Type | Set During | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| `icp.negative` | JSON object | Setup Z5b | nurge-icp, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
| `icp.signals` | JSON array | Setup Z6b | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-outbound, nurge-pipeline, nurge-analytics |
| `icp.technographics` | string | Setup Z5 (enriched) | nurge-icp, nurge-research |
| `product.competitors` | JSON array | Setup Z7b | nurge-product, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-replies, nurge-analytics |
| `product.objections` | JSON array | Setup Z8b | nurge-product, nurge-replies, nurge-outbound |
| `product.pricing_stance` | string | Setup Z7b | nurge-product, nurge-replies |
| `voice` | JSON object | Setup Z10b + learned over time | nurge-voice, nurge-outbound, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-sequence, nurge-review |
| `sequence.architecture` | JSON object | Setup Z9 | nurge-sequence |
| `cta.default_style` | string | Setup Z9 | nurge-outbound, nurge-sequence, nurge-analytics |

---

## Skill Interaction Map (Updated)

```
                            ┌─────────────┐
                            │ nurge-voice  │ ← NEW: referenced by all email-writing skills
                            └──────┬───────┘
                                   │
   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌───┴──────┐
   │ product  │  │   icp    │  │ personas │
   │(+compete)│  │(+signals)│  │(+objections)│
   └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘
        │              │             │
        └──────┬───────┴─────────────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴──────┐
        │  research   │ ← signal detection + competitive intel
        └──────┬──────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴──────┐
        │   qualify   │ ← signal scoring + disqualification
        └──────┬──────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴──────┐
        │  outbound   │ ← voice-matched + signal hooks + CTA strategy
        └──────┬──────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴──────┐
        │  sequence   │ ← 5-touch architecture + angle rotation
        └──────┬──────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴──────┐     ┌──────────┐
        │  followup   │     │  replies  │ ← battlecard-powered objection handling
        └──────┬──────┘     └────┬─────┘
               │                 │
               └────────┬────────┘
                        │
                 ┌──────┴──────┐
                 │    book     │
                 └─────────────┘

   ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐
   │ pipeline │  │   prospects   │ ← signal-based prioritization + negative ICP filtering
   └──────────┘  └───────────────┘

   ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
   │ hygiene  │  │ sending-health│  │analytics │  │  review  │
   └──────────┘  └───────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
                                    ← per-signal   ← voice drift
                                      reply rates    + objection
                                                     patterns
```

## Research Sources

- [Autobound: Complete Guide to Cold Email 2026](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/cold-email-guide-2026) — signal-based strategies, 15-25% reply rates
- [Autobound: 15 Sales Trigger Events](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/sales-trigger-events-templates) — trigger event taxonomy and templates
- [Autobound: Signal-Based Selling Guide](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/signal-based-selling-guide) — 644 signals, prioritization framework
- [Instantly: Cold Email Benchmark Report 2026](https://instantly.ai/cold-email-benchmark-report-2026) — 3.43% avg reply rate, follow-up data
- [Lavender: Best Length Cold Email](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/best-length-cold-email) — 25-50 words optimal
- [Lavender: Cold Email 101](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/cold-email-101) — reading level, I/You ratio, voice profiling
- [Lavender: Personalization Process](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/how-to-build-a-cold-email-personalization-process) — personality-based personalization
- [Gong: Surprising CTA](https://www.gong.io/blog/this-surprising-cold-email-cta-will-help-you-book-a-lot-more-meetings) — interest CTA > meeting CTA
- [Josh Braun: 4-T Framework](https://joshbraun.com/learn/cold-email/) — Truth, Trigger, Third-party, Tell
- [Josh Braun: Objection Prevention](https://joshbraun.com/diffusesalesobjections/) — prevent, don't handle
- [Apollo: ICP + Personas + Signals Onboarding](https://www.saasboost.io/blog/apollo-io-onboarding-document---using-icps-personas-messaging-signals-to-set-up-and-automated-outbound-engine) — structured ICP collection
- [Apollo: ICP Meaning in Sales](https://www.apollo.io/insights/icp-meaning-sales) — negative ICP, disqualification
- [SalesHandy: Cold Email Personalization 2026](https://www.saleshandy.com/blog/how-to-personalize-cold-emails/) — personalization depth vs reply rates
- [SalesHandy: Cold Email CTA](https://www.saleshandy.com/blog/cold-email-cta-to-book-more-meetings/) — CTA best practices

## Files to Update

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-zero.md` | Add Z5b, Z6b, Z7b, Z8b, replace Z9, add Z10b |
| `internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-experimenting.md` | Mirror new steps with data-hybrid approach |

| `docs/skills/skill-catalog.md` | Full rewrite with v2 data model |

toolUseResult.content:
# Skill Catalog v2: Research-Backed Data Model

**Date:** 2026-03-25
**Status:** Draft
**Context:** Based on research into Lavender, Apollo, Instantly, Autobound, Clay, Gong, Josh Braun, and 2026 cold email benchmarks.

## Problem

The current 17 skills are structurally sound but data-thin. Setup collects enough to generate *functional* skills but not *effective* ones. Research shows the gap between 3.4% reply rates (generic) and 25%+ reply rates (signal-based, voice-matched, objection-aware) comes down to what data the system knows before writing the first email.

## What Changes

- **1 new skill:** `nurge-voice` (sender personality profile)
- **9 enriched skills:** product, icp, personas, outbound, sequence, qualify, replies, analytics, review
- **7 new setup steps** in Phase 2 Zero flow
- **9 new config keys**
- Total: **18 skills** (17 project-local + 1 global setup)
- Core skills generated during setup: **6** (was 5)

## The 18 Skills

### Master Table

| # | Skill | Category | When Generated | What It Knows (Current) | What's New (v2) |
|---|-------|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | `nurge-setup` | Setup | Binary install | Onboarding orchestrator | +7 new collection steps |
| 1 | `nurge-product` | Context | Setup (core) | Company, positioning, social proof | **+competitors, +objections, +pricing stance, +differentiators** |
| 2 | `nurge-icp` | Context | Setup (core) | Company size, industry, stage, geo | **+negative ICP, +trigger signals, +technographics** |
| 3 | `nurge-personas` | Context | Setup (core) | Title, pain, messaging angle | **+per-persona objections, +CTA style, +tone profile, +buying role** |
| 4 | `nurge-voice` | Context | **Setup (core) — NEW** | — | **Writing style, tone, vocabulary, anti-patterns, sign-off, reading level** |
| 5 | `nurge-outbound` | Action | Setup (core) | 50-90 words, structure, filler rules | **+voice matching, +signal hooks, +CTA strategy, +reading level, +subject line rules** |
| 6 | `nurge-pipeline` | Workflow | Setup (core) | KPIs, sending limits | +signal-based prioritization |
| 7 | `nurge-research` | Research | Lazy (first use) | 5-layer parallel dossier | +signal detection, +competitive intel check |
| 8 | `nurge-qualify` | Research | Lazy | BANT, ICP fit, classification | **+disqualification criteria, +signal scoring tiers, +competitor detection** |
| 9 | `nurge-sequence` | Action | Lazy | 3-step cadence | **+architecture (5 touches), +angle rotation, +breakup email, +multi-threading** |
| 10 | `nurge-followup` | Action | Lazy | New-angle follow-ups | +objection-aware angles, +signal-based hooks |
| 11 | `nurge-replies` | Action | Lazy | Reply classification + response | **+competitor mention handling, +objection battlecard, +deflection handling** |
| 12 | `nurge-book` | Action | Lazy | Meeting booking | No change |
| 13 | `nurge-prospects` | Prospects | Lazy | List building, segmentation | +signal filtering, +negative ICP exclusion |
| 14 | `nurge-hygiene` | Health | Lazy | Data cleanup, compliance | No change |
| 15 | `nurge-sending-health` | Health | Lazy | DNS, warmup, bounces | No change |
| 16 | `nurge-analytics` | Measure | Lazy | KPI tracking | **+per-signal reply rate, +per-CTA conversion, +voice consistency** |
| 17 | `nurge-review` | Measure | Lazy | Weekly coaching | **+signal effectiveness, +voice drift, +objection patterns** |

### Generation Order

| Phase | Skills (in order) | Rationale |
|-------|-------------------|-----------|
| Setup (core 6) | product → icp → personas → voice → outbound → pipeline | Knowledge base first, then how to sound, then how to write, then how to orchestrate |
| Lazy (11) | Generated on first invocation | More data available by then = better skills |

---

## Skill Details

### 1. `nurge-product` — Company Battlecard

**Purpose:** Everything Claude needs about the sender's company to write accurate emails and handle conversations.

**Current contents:**
- Company name, website, industry
- One-line positioning for cold outreach
- Value propositions
- Social proof (or "none")

**New contents:**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Top 3-5 competitors** | Skills need competitors to "plant landmines" — features competitors can't match. Never name competitors proactively in emails. | Setup Z7b |
| **Differentiators per competitor** | "When they mention X, we do Y better because Z" — feeds nurge-replies objection handling | Setup Z7b |
| **Objection map (top 5)** | Pre-map every objection with pre-emptive framings. Josh Braun: prevent objections, don't handle them after the fact | Setup Z8b |
| **Pricing stance** | Not the actual price — the *framing*. "$250/mo" vs "replaces a $5K/mo SDR" vs "free trial, no card" | Setup Z7b |
| **What you're NOT** | "We're not a CRM. We're not Instantly." Prevents positioning drift in generated emails | Setup Z7b |

**Config keys:** `company.*`, `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `product.competitors` (JSON), `product.objections` (JSON), `product.pricing_stance`

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-replies, nurge-followup, nurge-qualify, nurge-research

---

### 2. `nurge-icp` — Who to Target (and Who Not To)

**Purpose:** Defines the ideal customer profile — which companies to target, which to avoid, and what signals indicate they're ready to buy.

**Current contents:**
- Company size, industry, stage, geography

**New contents:**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Negative ICP (disqualification)** | Characteristics that predict churn, slow cycles, or low LTV. Hard stop, not deprioritization. Apollo data: negative ICP is as valuable as positive ICP | Setup Z5b |
| **Trigger events / buying signals** | Signal-based outreach = 15-25% reply rates vs 3.4% generic. The single biggest lever for reply rates. Autobound: 644 signals from 29 sources | Setup Z6b |
| **Signal priority ranking** | Which signals indicate highest intent for *this specific* product. Funding matters more for some products, hiring for others | Setup Z6b |
| **Technographics** | Tech stack signals (uses React, runs on AWS, has Salesforce) indicate fit and determine approach angle | Setup Z5 (enriched) |
| **Timing windows** | Autobound data: reaching out within 48 hours of a trigger = 5x more likely to win. Define urgency per signal type | Setup Z6b |

**Config keys:** `icp.*`, `icp.negative` (JSON), `icp.signals` (JSON array), `icp.technographics`, `prospects.geography`

**Used by:** nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects, nurge-outbound, nurge-pipeline, nurge-analytics

---

### 3. `nurge-personas` — Who to Message and How

**Purpose:** Buyer personas — which people to message within target companies, and how to message each one differently.

**Current contents (per persona):**
- Title/role patterns
- Pain points
- Messaging angle

**New contents (per persona):**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | Collected During |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-persona objections** | CTOs object differently than VPs. "Too technical" vs "not proven enough" vs "we already use X" | Derived from objection map + persona during setup |
| **Per-persona CTA style** | Gong data: interest CTAs ("worth a look?") outperform meeting CTAs. But CTOs may prefer low-friction while VPs may respond to specific times | Setup (CTA step) |
| **Tone profile** | Technical/casual for CTOs, business/formal for VPs. Lavender: tone match to recipient personality = higher replies | Setup |
| **Buying role** | Champion (sells internally), Decision-maker (signs off), Blocker (can kill deal), Influencer (shapes opinion). Changes the ask | Setup Z6 (enriched) |
| **Per-persona positioning** | Different one-liner per persona. Already happened organically in the test onboarding — formalize it | Setup Z7 (enriched) |

**Config keys:** `personas` (enriched JSON array — each object gains: `objections`, `cta_style`, `tone`, `buying_role`, `positioning`)

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-sequence, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-book, nurge-research

---

### 4. `nurge-voice` — Sender Personality Profile (NEW)

**Purpose:** Makes every email sound like the user wrote it, not like AI. This is Nurge's long-term moat — the voice model gets better with every interaction.

**What it contains:**

| Data Point | How It's Extracted | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Formality level** | Analyze sent emails for contractions, greetings, structure | "Casual — contractions, first names, no 'Dear'" |
| **Sentence structure** | Avg sentence length, complexity, fragments | "Short sentences. Rarely uses semicolons. Fragments OK." |
| **Vocabulary patterns** | Frequently used words and phrases | "Says 'ship' not 'deliver'. Says 'worth a look' not 'I'd love to schedule'" |
| **Anti-patterns** | Things the user would NEVER say | "Never: 'Hope this finds you well', 'Just circling back', 'Per my last email'" |
| **Opener style** | How they start emails | "Jumps straight in. No greeting pleasantries. First name only." |
| **Closer style** | How they sign off | "Just first name. No title. No phone number in signature." |
| **Punctuation / emoji** | Habits | "Uses em dashes. No emojis. Occasional '...' for trailing thoughts" |
| **Reading level** | Lavender data: 3rd-5th grade reading level = 67% more replies | "Grade 4 — simple words, short sentences" |
| **I/You ratio** | Lavender data: more 'you' language = more replies | "Target: 3:1 you-to-I ratio" |
| **Humor / personality** | Whether they're playful, dry, or strictly professional | "Dry, direct. Occasional wit. Never cheesy or forced." |

**How it's built:**
1. **During setup (Z10b):** Analyze classified outbound + personal emails for patterns. Even 1 outbound + 10 personal emails yield a useful baseline.
2. **Over time:** Every draft the user edits, rejects, or approves refines the profile. Rejected phrasings become anti-patterns. Accepted ones reinforce the model. This is the "Whoop effect" — the system gets better the longer you use it.

**Config keys:** `voice.formality`, `voice.sentence_style`, `voice.vocabulary`, `voice.anti_patterns`, `voice.opener`, `voice.closer`, `voice.reading_level`, `voice.personality` (or single `voice` JSON object)

**Used by:** nurge-outbound, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-sequence, nurge-book, nurge-review (drift detection)

---

### 5. `nurge-outbound` — Write Cold Emails

**Purpose:** Draft personalized cold emails. The highest-priority action skill.

**Current rules:**
- 50-90 word hard limit
- Structure: 1 hook → 1-2 value → 1 CTA
- No filler words
- Persona-specific tone
- Social proof weaving (if exists)

**New rules:**

| Rule | Research Backing |
|---|---|
| **Match sender voice profile** | Lavender: emails matching sender's natural voice get 4x reply rates. Reference nurge-voice for every draft |
| **Signal-based hook (sentence 1)** | Autobound: signal-based personalization = 15-25% reply rate. "Saw you just raised Series B" > "Hi, I'm from Company X" |
| **Interest CTA by default** | Gong: "Worth a look?" outperforms "Free Tuesday at 2?" — asking for time triggers loss aversion. Override per persona if configured |
| **Reading level: grade 3-5** | Lavender: 3rd-5th grade reading level = 67% more replies. Simple words, short sentences |
| **I/You ratio: 3:1 you-to-I** | Lavender: more "you" = more replies. Never open a sentence with "I" |
| **Subject line: 36-50 chars** | Instantly benchmark: 36-50 char subjects = highest response. Numbers boost open rate 113% |
| **Competitor-aware positioning** | Never name competitors. Plant "landmines" — features they can't match. Reference nurge-product.competitors |
| **25-50 words is the new sweet spot** | Lavender 2026 data: 25-50 words optimal. Our 50-90 range may be too generous. Consider tightening. |

**Updated email structure:**
```
1. Signal-based hook (1 sentence) — references a trigger event or specific observation about the prospect
2. Value prop (1-2 sentences) — connects THEIR situation to YOUR solution. Focus on outcome, not features
3. CTA (1 sentence) — interest-based by default. Low friction. Close-ended question
Total: 50-90 words. Reading level: grade 3-5. Voice: match sender profile from nurge-voice.
```

**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `product.competitors`, `personas`, `voice.*`, `icp.signals`, `prospects.geography`, `sending.*`, `cta.default_style`

---

### 6. `nurge-pipeline` — Daily Orchestrator

**Purpose:** Manages the full outbound workflow, decides next actions, coordinates all skills.

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Signal-based prioritization** | Prospects with active signals go first. 2-3 stacked signals = highest priority tier. Autobound: multi-signal = 25-40% reply rate |
| **Signal freshness decay** | Funding from yesterday > funding from 3 weeks ago. 48-hour window = 5x conversion. Show signal age |
| **Sequence health check** | "5 prospects stuck at step 2 for >7 days" — suggest moving on or trying different angle |

**Prioritization tiers (from Autobound framework):**

| Tier | Signals | Action | Expected Reply Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 (Hot) | 2-3+ active signals | Immediate, fully personalized, assign to best outreach | 25-40% |
| Tier 2 (Warm) | 1 active signal | Semi-personalized cadence | 10-15% |
| Tier 3 (Cold) | 0 signals, fits ICP | Batch cadence, lighter personalization | 3-5% |

---

### 7. `nurge-research` — Prospect Intelligence

**Purpose:** Deep-dive research on a target company or prospect before writing outreach.

**Current:** 5-layer parallel research (web, website, enrichment, public profiles, prior interactions).

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Signal detection** | During research, actively look for trigger events matching `icp.signals`. Flag which signals are active and how fresh they are |
| **Competitive intel check** | Check if prospect uses a competitor (from `product.competitors`). Changes the messaging angle entirely — displacement vs greenfield |
| **Signal freshness** | Always flag recency: "Raised $5M (2 weeks ago)" vs "Raised $5M (6 months ago)". Stale signals ≠ no signals, but urgency drops |
| **Personality indicators** | From LinkedIn posts, podcast appearances, blog writing — communication style clues to help voice-match the email |

---

### 8. `nurge-qualify` — Score and Filter

**Purpose:** Evaluate whether a lead is worth pursuing. Classify, score, and route.

**Current:** Classify (prospect/competitor/partnership) → BANT scoring.

**New additions:**

| Addition | Why |
|---|---|
| **Disqualification criteria** | Explicit "do NOT pursue" from `icp.negative`. Hard stop, not deprioritization. Apollo: negative ICP prevents wasted cycles |
| **Signal scoring** | 0 signals = cold, 1 = warm, 2+ = hot. Determines outreach tier, personalization level, and urgency |
| **Competitor user detection** | If they use a competitor → displacement opportunity (different messaging) vs if they use nothing → greenfield opportunity |
| **Budget reality check** | Apollo: firmographic match ≠ ability to buy. Check for budget signals (funding, hiring, growth rate) |

**Updated output tiers:**

| Tier | Criteria | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot** | 2+ active signals, strong ICP fit | Immediate outreach, full personalization, deep research |
| **Warm** | 1 active signal or strong ICP fit | Semi-personalized sequence within the week |
| **Cold** | ICP fit but no signals | Batch cadence, monitor for signals |
| **Nurture** | Good fit but bad timing | Park and monitor. Re-qualify when signals appear |
| **Disqualified** | Matches negative ICP or risk signal | Do not email. Log reason |

---

### 9. `nurge-sequence` — Multi-Touch Cadences

**Purpose:** Design and manage multi-step outreach sequences.

**Current:** Simple 3-touch sequence.

**New: Full sequence architecture.**

| Addition | Research Backing |
|---|---|
| **5-touch default** | Instantly: 58% of positive replies from email #1, but 60% of ALL positives come from follow-ups. More touches = more chances |
| **Angle rotation** | Each touch uses a DIFFERENT angle. Never "just following up." New pain point, new proof, new framing per touch |
| **Breakup email (touch 5)** | Josh Braun: low-pressure exit ("Not the right time? No problem.") sometimes triggers the reply that direct asks didn't |
| **Multi-threading** | Same company, different person. If CTO doesn't reply after 3 touches, try VP Eng. Apollo approach |
| **Timing by persona** | CTOs: shorter sequence (3 touches, tighter spacing). VPs: standard (5 touches). SDRs: fastest cadence |

**Default sequence architecture:**

| Touch | Day | Purpose | Angle | Word Count |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Day 0 | Signal-based hook + primary value prop | Primary pain point | 50-90 |
| 2 | Day 3 | New angle, different value | Secondary pain point or proof | 40-70 |
| 3 | Day 7 | Social proof or case study | Third-party validation | 40-70 |
| 4 | Day 14 | Direct ask or escalate | More specific/direct CTA | 30-50 |
| 5 | Day 21 | Breakup | "No worries if not — just wanted to flag it" | 20-40 |

**Config it reads:** `personas`, `kpi.*`, `sending.*`, `voice.*`, `product.social_proof`, `icp.signals`, `sequence.architecture`, `cta.default_style`

---

### 10. `nurge-followup` — Non-Response Follow-Ups

**Purpose:** Write follow-up emails for prospects who haven't replied.

**Enriched with:**
- **Genuinely new angle per touch** — not restating email #1. Different pain point, different proof, different framing
- **Voice consistency** — must match nurge-voice profile
- **Signal-based hooks** — if a NEW signal has occurred since last touch, lead with it ("Just saw you launched X...")
- **Shorter than initial** — follow-ups should be 30-50 words, not 50-90
- **Objection-aware** — if the silence pattern suggests a likely objection (e.g., pricing), pre-emptively address it

---

### 11. `nurge-replies` — Handle Incoming Responses

**Purpose:** Classify and respond to incoming replies.

**Current:** Classify (interested/objection/not now/not interested/referral/OOO) + draft response.

**New: Battlecard-powered objection handling.**

| Reply Type | New Handling | Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **"We use [competitor]"** | Pull competitor battlecard from nurge-product. Respond with differentiators. Never trash the competitor — acknowledge they're good, then differentiate | `product.competitors` |
| **"Too expensive / no budget"** | Pull pricing stance. Reframe value ("replaces a $5K/mo SDR"), don't discount. Offer a trial if available | `product.pricing_stance`, `product.objections` |
| **"Not the right time"** | Acknowledge + ask "when would be better?" Schedule nurture follow-up. Don't push | `product.objections` |
| **"Send me more info"** | This is usually a deflection. Send a one-pager, not a feature dump. Ask a qualifying question back to test real interest | `product.objections` |
| **"How is this different from X?"** | Pull specific differentiators. Be honest about where competitor is stronger — builds credibility | `product.competitors` |
| **Positive reply** | Move to booking. Match their energy/tone. Don't oversell — they already said yes | Standard |

---

### 12. `nurge-book` — Meeting Booking

No changes. Current design is solid.

---

### 13. `nurge-prospects` — List Building & CRM

**New additions:**
- **Signal-based filtering:** Prioritize prospects with active trigger events from `icp.signals`
- **Negative ICP exclusion:** Auto-filter against `icp.negative` disqualification criteria
- **Signal stacking score:** Show signal count per prospect for prioritization display

---

### 14. `nurge-hygiene` — Data Cleanup

No changes needed.

### 15. `nurge-sending-health` — Infrastructure Monitoring

No changes needed.

---

### 16. `nurge-analytics` — Performance Tracking

**New metrics:**

| Metric | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Reply rate by signal type** | "Funding signals → 22% reply rate, hiring signals → 8%." Invest research time where signals actually work |
| **Reply rate by CTA type** | "Interest CTAs → 12%, specific time CTAs → 6%." Optimize CTA strategy per persona |
| **Voice consistency score** | Are generated emails drifting from the sender's profile? Flag drift before it becomes a pattern |
| **Per-competitor win/loss** | When prospects use competitor X, how often do we convert? Informs competitive positioning |
| **Signal ROI** | Which signals are worth the research time vs noise. Stop chasing signals that don't convert |

---

### 17. `nurge-review` — Weekly Coaching

**New coaching areas:**

| Area | What It Does |
|---|---|
| **Signal effectiveness** | "This week, funding signals drove 3 replies but hiring signals drove 0. Deprioritize hiring signal research." |
| **Voice drift detection** | "Your last 5 emails are more formal than your usual style. Intentional?" |
| **Objection pattern detection** | "3 prospects said 'too expensive' this week — consider adjusting pricing framing in initial emails" |
| **Competitor intelligence** | "2 prospects mentioned using [competitor] this week. Here's what worked / didn't in those conversations" |
| **Sequence optimization** | "Touch 2 is getting 0 replies. The angle isn't working — here are 3 alternatives" |

---

## New Setup Steps (Phase 2 Zero)

These steps are inserted into the existing Zero flow:

| New Step | Insert After | What to Collect | Time Added |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z5b: Negative ICP** | Z5 (ICP) | "Who should you NOT waste time on?" AI suggests based on product + ICP. Company types, persona anti-patterns, deal-breakers | ~1 min |
| **Z6b: Trigger signals** | Z6 (Personas) | "What signals tell you a company is ready to buy right now?" AI suggests top 5 based on product. User confirms/ranks | ~2 min |
| **Z7b: Competitive landscape** | Z7 (Positioning) | "Who else solves this problem?" Top 3-5 competitors or alternatives. Differentiators. "What do you do better?" Pricing stance | ~2 min |
| **Z8b: Objection map** | Z8 (Social proof) | "What pushback do you expect when you reach out?" AI suggests top 5 based on product + competitors. Pre-emptive framings | ~1 min |
| **Z9: CTA + sequence strategy** | Replaces old Z9 (BANT) | Default CTA style. Sequence length preference. Breakup style. Replace BANT (useless for zero users) with something actionable | ~1 min |
| **Z10b: Voice extraction** | Before Z11 (Skills) | Analyze classified outbound + personal emails for writing patterns. Present profile for user review | ~1 min |

**Total added time:** ~8 minutes. Total setup: ~13 minutes (was ~5).

**Tradeoff:** 8 extra minutes during setup → dramatically better skills from day 1. Every email after setup benefits.

---

## New Config Keys

| Key | Type | Set During | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| `icp.negative` | JSON object | Setup Z5b | nurge-icp, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
| `icp.signals` | JSON array | Setup Z6b | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-outbound, nurge-pipeline, nurge-analytics |
| `icp.technographics` | string | Setup Z5 (enriched) | nurge-icp, nurge-research |
| `product.competitors` | JSON array | Setup Z7b | nurge-product, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-replies, nurge-analytics |
| `product.objections` | JSON array | Setup Z8b | nurge-product, nurge-replies, nurge-outbound |
| `product.pricing_stance` | string | Setup Z7b | nurge-product, nurge-replies |
| `voice` | JSON object | Setup Z10b + learned over time | nurge-voice, nurge-outbound, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-sequence, nurge-review |
| `sequence.architecture` | JSON object | Setup Z9 | nurge-sequence |
| `cta.default_style` | string | Setup Z9 | nurge-outbound, nurge-sequence, nurge-analytics |

---

## Skill Interaction Map (Updated)

```
                            ┌─────────────┐
                            │ nurge-voice  │ ← NEW: referenced by all email-writing skills
                            └──────┬───────┘
                                   │
   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌───┴──────┐
   │ product  │  │   icp    │  │ personas │
   │(+compete)│  │(+signals)│  │(+objections)│
   └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘
        │              │             │
        └──────┬───────┴─────────────┘

## Research Sources

- [Autobound: Complete Guide to Cold Email 2026](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/cold-email-guide-2026) — signal-based strategies, 15-25% reply rates
- [Autobound: 15 Sales Trigger Events](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/sales-trigger-events-templates) — trigger event taxonomy and templates
- [Autobound: Signal-Based Selling Guide](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/signal-based-selling-guide) — 644 signals, prioritization framework
- [Instantly: Cold Email Benchmark Report 2026](https://instantly.ai/cold-email-benchmark-report-2026) — 3.43% avg reply rate, follow-up data
- [Lavender: Best Length Cold Email](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/best-length-cold-email) — 25-50 words optimal
- [Lavender: Cold Email 101](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/cold-email-101) — reading level, I/You ratio, voice profiling
- [Lavender: Personalization Process](https://www.lavender.ai/blog/how-to-build-a-cold-email-personalization-process) — personality-based personalization
- [Gong: Surprising CTA](https://www.gong.io/blog/this-surprising-cold-email-cta-will-help-you-book-a-lot-more-meetings) — interest CTA > meeting CTA
- [Josh Braun: 4-T Framework](https://joshbraun.com/learn/cold-email/) — Truth, Trigger, Third-party, Tell
- [Josh Braun: Objection Prevention](https://joshbraun.com/diffusesalesobjections/) — prevent, don't handle
- [Apollo: ICP + Personas + Signals Onboarding](https://www.saasboost.io/blog/apollo-io-onboarding-document---using-icps-personas-messaging-signals-to-set-up-and-automated-outbound-engine) — structured ICP collection
- [Apollo: ICP Meaning in Sales](https://www.apollo.io/insights/icp-meaning-sales) — negative ICP, disqualification
- [SalesHandy: Cold Email Personalization 2026](https://www.saleshandy.com/blog/how-to-personalize-cold-emails/) — personalization depth vs reply rates
- [SalesHandy: Cold Email CTA](https://www.saleshandy.com/blog/cold-email-cta-to-book-more-meetings/) — CTA best practices
